lecture_context.lecture_title: Lecture 4 - Word Vectors and Language Models

unit.name: Word2vec variants and negative sampling
unit.description: Covers why word2vec keeps two vectors, the skip-gram and CBOW family, naive softmax, and negative sampling with sigmoid losses.
unit.summary: After warning students not to follow an old assignment that asked for a from-scratch word2vec implementation, the lecturer answers why word2vec uses two vector sets: using the same vector for center and outside words creates a quadratic self-term when the center word appears in the normalization, making the math messier; disjoint vectors keep the derivation simple and are usually averaged afterward [ts=1537s]. The original Mikolov family is framed as two model structures: skip-gram predicts surrounding words from an inside word, while continuous bag of words predicts the center word from its context [ts=1622s]. The training objective previously presented is naive softmax, which sums over every vocabulary word, requiring many dot products and exponentiations for large vocabularies [ts=1662s]. Negative sampling replaces full softmax with simple logistic regressions: make the true context word high probability and a few randomly sampled words low probability [ts=1741s]. The sigmoid/logistic function maps real-valued dot products to probabilities from 0 to 1, making positive dot products favorable for real outside words and unfavorable for negative samples [ts=1810s]. Negative examples are not sampled uniformly; word2vec uses unigram probabilities raised to the 3/4 power, which increases the relative chance of less frequent words while still favoring common words [ts=1924s].

key_points.text: Using separate center and outside vectors avoids a messy quadratic self-term in the softmax normalization.
The word2vec family includes skip-gram, which predicts context from center, and CBOW, which predicts center from context.
Naive softmax sums over every vocabulary word, which is expensive for vocabularies with hundreds of thousands of words.
Negative sampling trains the model to like the true context word and dislike a small number of sampled non-context words.
The logistic sigmoid maps any real dot product to a probability between 0 and 1.
Negative samples are drawn from unigram probability raised to the 3/4 power, partially flattening the frequency distribution.

The original Mikolov family is framed as two model structures: skip-gram predicts surrounding words from an inside word, while continuous bag of words predicts the center word from its context [ts=1622s]. The training objective previously presented is naive softmax, which sums over every vocabulary word, requiring many dot products and exponentiations for large vocabularies [ts=1662s]. Negative sampling replaces full softmax with simple logistic regressions: make the true context word high probability and a few randomly sampled words low probability [ts=1741s]. The sigmoid/logistic function maps real-valued dot products to probabilities from 0 to 1, making positive dot products favorable for real outside words and unfavorable for negative samples [ts=1810s]. Negative examples are not sampled uniformly; word2vec uses unigram probabilities raised to the 3/4 power, which increases the relative chance of less frequent words while still favoring common words [ts=1924s].

kp_catalog.name: Two-vector word2vec parameterization
kp_catalog.description: Word2vec usually learns separate center and outside vectors and averages or otherwise combines them after training.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because two-vector word2vec parameterization clarifies word2vec usually learns separate center and outside vectors and averages or otherwise combines them after training, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Naive softmax vs word2vec model variants
kp_catalog.description: Word2vec includes skip-gram and CBOW structures, and naive softmax computes a full vocabulary normalization.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because naive softmax vs word2vec model variants clarifies word2vec includes skip-gram and CBOW structures, and naive softmax computes a full vocabulary normalization, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Negative sampling with sigmoid objectives
kp_catalog.description: Negative sampling replaces full softmax with logistic decisions that distinguish true context words from sampled noise words.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because negative sampling with sigmoid objectives formalizes how negative sampling replaces full softmax with logistic decisions that distinguish true context words from sampled noise words, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Unigram distribution to the 3/4 power
kp_catalog.description: The negative-sampling distribution uses unigram probabilities raised to 3/4 to upweight less frequent words relative to raw frequency.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because unigram distribution to the 3/4 power clarifies the negative-sampling distribution uses unigram probabilities raised to 3/4 to upweight less frequent words relative to raw frequency, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
